metadata.seed: I was doing okay, but, I mean, I got comfortable with that extra income, and so now my fear is that I've gotten too comfortable with it, and now that it's not there anymore, it's going to affect the fact I'm not going to be able to pay for food or my rent.

patient.query: I was doing okay, but, I mean, I got comfortable with that extra income, and so now my fear is that I've gotten too comfortable with it, and now that it's not there anymore, it's going to affect the fact I'm not going to be able to pay for food or my rent.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not going to be able to pay for food
I'm not going to be able to pay for rent

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how unsettling it feels to suddenly lose that extra income and worry about meeting basic needs. It’s understandable that this shift can stir a sense of losing control and feeling like a burden. What thoughts or worries come up most when you think about food and rent right now?

patient.query: Yeah, it's just really overwhelming. I keep thinking about how tight things are getting and what happens if I can’t pay the rent. It makes my stomach drop just imagining it. And then there's food... I just worry about having enough. It's like this constant weight on my mind, and I feel like I’m failing somehow.

automatic_thoughts: what happens if I can’t pay the rent
I’m failing somehow

llm_response.response: It sounds like the constant worry about rent and food is making you feel like you’re losing control and overwhelmed. Those thoughts can feel heavy and scary, especially when you imagine the worst. What’s one small thing you could do right now to ease that feeling, even if it’s just a brief pause or a quick check of your budget?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel pretty heavy. I guess I could look at my budget, but honestly, I’m kind of afraid of what I’ll find. Every time I check, it just seems worse, and it makes me feel so defeated. I want to be proactive, but I also don’t want to face that reality right now. It’s like I’m stuck between wanting to know and just wanting to ignore it.

automatic_thoughts: I'm afraid of what I'll find
It just seems worse
I feel defeated
I don't want to face that reality

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response:
It’s understandable that the idea of looking at the budget feels heavy and scary, especially when you worry about what it might mean for you.  
You could try setting a very short, specific time—just a few minutes—to glance at one part of the budget, so the task feels more manageable.  
When you feel defeated, reminding yourself that facing the numbers can actually lessen the sense of burden you fear might help shift the experience.

patient.query: I get that… but it still feels like I’m just putting off the inevitable. I’ve tried that before, just looking at a little bit, but then I end up spiraling anyway. It’s like, once I start thinking about it, I can't stop. I just feel so anxious, and then I convince myself that I'm just going to mess it all up even more. I wish there was an easier way to deal with this weight.

triggers: trying to look at a little bit
automatic_thoughts: I'm just putting off the inevitable
I can't stop
I'm going to mess it all up even more
I wish there was an easier way to deal with this weight

llm_response.response: It sounds like the anxiety feels almost inevitable, and that can make it hard to stop the cycle of spiraling thoughts. When you notice the “I’ll mess it up” voice, try to pause and ask yourself what evidence you have that this outcome is certain. Breaking the situation into a few tiny, concrete actions—like setting a 5‑minute timer to check a single food item—can help you feel more in control and reduce the sense of overwhelm.

patient.query: It feels really heavy. I try to look at my budget, but I'm scared of what I'll see. Every time I do, it just seems worse, and I end up feeling so defeated. I want to be proactive, but facing that reality is hard. I feel stuck between wanting to know and wanting to ignore it. When I start thinking about it, I can’t stop, and it just makes me anxious. I end up convincing myself I’m going to mess everything up, and I wish

automatic_thoughts: I'm scared of what I'll see
it just seems worse
I'm going to mess everything up

llm_response.response: It sounds like looking at the budget feels like a looming threat that makes you feel out of control and anxious. You’re caught between wanting to face the numbers and fearing that seeing them will overwhelm you. A small, manageable step—perhaps reviewing just one line or setting a short timer—might help you feel less stuck and more in charge.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess that does make sense. It’s like the idea of looking at my budget turns into this monster in my head. I know I should face it, but it just feels so daunting. A part of me wants to be brave and take control, but then I get this wave of panic. I don’t know if I can handle it right now. It’s just really hard to find that balance between facing the truth and protecting myself from feeling completely overwhelmed.

automatic_thoughts: it turns into a monster
I should face it
it feels daunting
I don’t know if I can handle it

llm_response.response: It sounds like the budget feels like a monster that’s hard to face, and that fear of losing control is amplifying the panic. Acknowledging that feeling can help you see it as a manageable challenge rather than an insurmountable one. You might try breaking the review into a few short, concrete steps—just a few minutes at a time—to keep the overwhelm at bay while still moving forward.

patient.query: It’s just so daunting to even think about looking at my budget. The idea turns into this monster in my head, and I just freeze. I want to be brave and take control, but then the panic sets in. I don’t know if I can handle it right now. It’s hard to find that balance between facing the truth and protecting myself from feeling completely overwhelmed.

automatic_thoughts: the idea turns into a monster
I just freeze
I don’t know if I can handle it
hard to find balance between facing truth and protecting myself

llm_response.response: It sounds like the idea of looking at your budget feels like a big, intimidating task that can trigger a lot of anxiety. That feeling of panic can make it hard to decide how to begin, so breaking it down into a single, small step—like reviewing just one line or category—might feel more doable. Remember, it’s okay to pause and regroup if you start to feel overwhelmed; you can always take a short break and return when you feel ready.

patient.query: It’s just so daunting to think about looking at my budget. The idea feels like this monster in my head, and I just freeze up. I really wish I could be brave and face it, but then I feel that wave of panic wash over me. It’s exhausting trying to find that balance between knowing I should look and just wanting to protect myself from the overwhelm. I don’t know how to make it feel less heavy.

triggers: thinking about looking at my budget
automatic_thoughts: it feels like a monster
I just freeze up
I wish I could be brave
it's exhausting
I want to protect myself from the overwhelm
I don't know how to make it feel less heavy

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy that thought feels, like a monster that makes you freeze. It’s understandable that the idea of looking at your budget triggers panic and a sense of overwhelm. Maybe we could try breaking it into a tiny, manageable step—just a quick glance at one line—so it feels less daunting and gives you a chance to test that bravery without the full weight.

patient.query: That actually makes sense... breaking it down like that. I mean, I guess if I just looked at one line, it might take some of that weight off. But even the thought of it still makes me anxious. I worry that seeing even a small number will just spiral me into that panic. I don't know how to stop that cycle once it starts. It just feels like a lot to deal with.

automatic_thoughts: it might take some of that weight off
seeing a small number will spiral me into panic
I don't know how to stop that cycle

neutral: Anxiety about body function or health (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the idea of seeing even a small number can feel like a trigger that pulls you into a wave of worry about your weight and how it might affect your health. You’re noticing how quickly that anxiety can spiral, and that can feel overwhelming. One approach might be to pause right when that thought starts and gently remind yourself that the number is just a piece of information, not a verdict on your worth or future. This small shift can help you break the cycle before it fully takes hold.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about body function or health
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Yeah, I see what you're saying. It makes sense that it’s just information, but when I’m in that moment of panic, it feels so personal. It’s like the number becomes a judgment on me, and it’s hard to separate the two. I really want to break that cycle; I just don’t know how to get to that point where I can pause and remind myself. Everything feels so intertwined, and it’s exhausting to think about trying to sort it all out.

automatic_thoughts: the number becomes a judgment on me
it's hard to separate the two
behaviors: struggle to pause and remind myself

neutral: Amnesia for day to day facts (finding)
contradiction: Able to make decision on safe information to give over telephone (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how exhausting it feels when panic makes the number feel like a judgment on you. It can help to pause just long enough to notice the breath or a simple sensation—like the feel of your feet on the floor—so you can remind yourself that the number is just information, not a verdict about you. Each brief pause is a small step toward breaking that cycle, and you can build on it gradually.
